Summary: APOE4 is described as the single greatest genetic risk factor for sporadic Alzheimer’s disease. In human isogenic iPSC-derived brain cells and organoids, APOE4 (role/context) causes cell-type-specific dysfunctions: in neurons it increases neuronal differentiation, synapse number and mEPSC frequency, enlarges early endosomes, and elevates Aβ42 secretion; in astrocytes it reduces APOE mRNA/protein and secretion, dysregulates lipid metabolism with intracellular and secreted cholesterol accumulation, and impairs Aβ42 uptake and lysosomal degradation; in microglia-like cells it alters morphology, up-regulates immune-related genes, and slows Aβ42 phagocytosis (mechanism). These cell-specific defects lead to increased extracellular Aβ aggregation and tau phosphorylation in six-month cerebral organoids (outcome). Converting APOE4 to APOE3 in sAD iPSCs was sufficient to attenuate many of these AD-related phenotypes.
References: Lin, Y. T., Seo, J., Gao, F., Feldman, H. M., Wen, H. L., Penney, J., Cam, H. P., Gjoneska, E., Raja, W. K., Cheng, J., Rueda, R., Kritskiy, O., Abdurrob, F., Peng, Z., Milo, B., Yu, C. J., Elmsaouri, S., Dey, D., Ko, T., … Tsai, L. H. (2018). APOE4 Causes Widespread Molecular and Cellular Alterations Associated with Alzheimer's Disease Phenotypes in Human iPSC-Derived Brain Cell Types. Neuron, 98(6), 1141-1154.e7. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.neuron.2018.05.008
